original_abstract: This article argues that the recent shift in practical theology toward "lived religion" provides a vital hermeneutic for understanding the impact of the Harry Potter series on adolescent spiritual formation and identity. As traditional institutional religion loses its hold, people are increasingly seeking meaning and purpose within sociocultural phenomena. By examining the Potter narrative through the lens of lived theology, this study demonstrates how the series functions as a contemporary "sacred space." Within this space, adolescents encounter core Christian values—such as sacrificial love and the importance of fellowship—expressed in a language that resonates with their own experiences, offering a transformative alternative to the dominant secular discourses of consumerism and materialism.
humanized_abstract: The article is grounded within a “lived religion” perspective of practical theology. With institutional religion quickly becoming less relevant in modern life, people are searching for meaningful experiences within social and cultural contexts. Bringing a lived theology perspective to bear within the narrative of the Harry Potter series this article illuminates the religious potential of popular culture that enables a space to emerge where teenagers can experience new dimensions of spiritual development which impacts their religious identity formation. Against the background of a culture that has given itself over to consumerist ideology, in which everyday life has become mundane and devoid of transcendence, the Harry Potter series functions as a “sacred space” within which teenagers experience the gospel of Christianity through the narrative vehicle of sacrificial love, as well as communal fellowship, both of which enable an embodied engagement with evangelical values in ways that affirms adolescent life worlds.
